issues.description: create_solution_map is a verbatim duplicate of the pre-existing build_solved_values helper defined immediately above at lines 55-74. Both functions take (params: &[AutoParam], x: &[f64]), assert equal lengths, zip the slices, and construct Value::Scalar { si_value, dimension: dimension_of(&param.param_type) } for each pair. The only differences are cosmetic: renamed parameters ('specifications'/'solution_values'/'computed_val' vs 'params'/'x'/'val'), a trailing comma in the assert, and a different doc comment. There is no semantic difference whatsoever. Shipping two functions with identical behavior directly contradicts the 'prefer minimal real implementations' convention and the spirit of the earlier task (#10954) that extracted build_solved_values precisely to deduplicate this loop.
issues.suggested_fix: Delete create_solution_map entirely and keep build_solved_values as the single helper. If the reviewer/task author prefers the newer naming, rename build_solved_values in place and update the three existing callsites (solver.rs:537, :662, :697) plus the four unit tests (lines 2335-2464) and the two integration-test comments (solver_integration.rs:629, :1496) — do not leave two copies.

issues.description: create_solution_map has zero callers anywhere in the workspace (grep across the repo returns no hits outside the definition itself). As a private free function with no `pub` visibility, this will either emit a `dead_code` warning or — given that reify-constraints typically builds with -D warnings — fail the clippy/lint gate used by the orchestrator's verification step. Even if it compiles, it adds dead surface area that future readers will have to reason about.
issues.suggested_fix: Remove the function. If it is intended to replace build_solved_values, do the rename and update all callers in the same change rather than introducing a second unused copy.

issues.description: No tests accompany the new function. build_solved_values has four dedicated unit tests (correctness, empty input, dimensionless type, length-mismatch panic) at lines 2335-2464. If create_solution_map were actually intended as a new API (which the duplication argument above says it shouldn't be), it would ship without equivalent coverage — the length-mismatch assert path in particular would be untested.
issues.suggested_fix: N/A once the function is deleted. If instead the team chooses to rename build_solved_values to create_solution_map, update the existing tests to cover the renamed function so coverage is preserved rather than duplicated.

issues.description: The surrounding helpers in this file follow a consistent `build_*` verb convention (build_solved_values, build_trial_values). Introducing a sibling named create_solution_map breaks that local naming pattern for no stated reason, and the verbose parameter names ('specifications', 'solution_values', 'computed_val') are inconsistent with the terse idioms used elsewhere in solver.rs (params/x/val, which match the Nelder-Mead literature and the rest of the module).
issues.suggested_fix: If a rename is desired, prefer something like `build_solution_values` to stay inside the build_* family, and keep the parameter names aligned with the rest of the file (params, x).

comprehensive_reviewer.summary: The diff adds a new private helper `create_solution_map` that is a line-for-line semantic duplicate of the existing `build_solved_values` helper defined immediately above it — same signature shape, same length assertion, same zip-and-map body, same Value::Scalar construction via dimension_of. The new function has zero callers anywhere in the workspace, so it is dead code that will also clash with the file's existing `build_*` naming convention and with the four unit tests already covering build_solved_values. This directly contradicts the earlier deduplication work (task #10954) that created build_solved_values in the first place. The change should either be dropped, or converted into an in-place rename that updates all three existing callsites and tests rather than introducing a second identical copy. No performance, concurrency, or error-handling regressions beyond the duplication itself.
